1: Haggai the prophet and Zechariah the prophet, a descendant of Iddo, prophesied to the Jews in Judah and Jerusalem, in the name of the God of Israel. Two prophets arise to break the building-cessation — the prophetic word as the catalyst for renewed covenant work. Christ is the fulfillment of both prophets: Haggai's promise of the 'latter glory' exceeding the former (Haggai 2:9) and Zechariah's vision of the Branch (Zechariah 3:8; Ezra 6:12) both converge on him. Where two or three prophets speak in YHWH's name, Christ is the one to whom all prophecy points (1 Peter 1:10-12).
2: Zerubbabel and Jeshua rose up and began to rebuild the house of God in Jerusalem, and the prophets of God were with them, supporting them. The prophetic word energizes the covenantal leadership; the rebuilding resumes. Christ's resurrection similarly re-energizes the disciples who had scattered at the arrest: 'beginning from Jerusalem, you will be my witnesses' (Acts 1:8). The prophetic word and the empowering presence together restart the covenant project.
3: At the same time Tattenai the governor of Beyond the River and Shethar-bozenai and their associates came to them and said: 'Who gave you a decree to build this house and to finish this timber work?' The interrogation by imperial officials — a new round of opposition as the building resumes. Christ also faced repeated official challenges: 'By what authority are you doing these things, and who gave you this authority?' (Matthew 21:23). The question of authorization is the recurring challenge to covenant building activity.
4: They also asked them this: 'What are the names of the men who are building this building?' The demand for names — the identification of the covenant builders for imperial record. Paul similarly gives his name to the authorities before whom he stands (Acts 22:3: 'I am a Jew, born in Tarsus'). The naming of covenant builders by imperial opposition is the precursor to the recording of their names in the Lamb's book of life (Revelation 21:27).
5: But the eye of their God was on the elders of the Jews, and they did not stop them until the report should reach Darius and then an answer of letter be returned. The eye of God watching over the builders prevents immediate cessation — the work continues during the imperial investigation. 'The eyes of the LORD are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good' (Proverbs 15:3); for the covenant builder, divine oversight is protection, not surveillance. Christ's promise: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20) — the watching eye becomes the accompanying presence.
6: The copy of the letter that Tattenai sent to Darius — the official report about the building being resumed. The imperial administrative machinery processing the renewal of the covenant project. Christ's ministry also generated official reports: Pilate wrote his assessment to the governor; the Sanhedrin's deliberations are recorded in John 11:47-53. The imperial record of covenant activity is itself a form of the testimony that will eventually reach all nations (Matthew 24:14).
7: 'To Darius the king, all peace.' The diplomatic opening of the letter. The covenant community's work is now officially in the communication stream of the Persian Empire. The church's witness eventually reaches the emperor himself through Paul (Acts 25:12: 'I appeal to Caesar'). The covenant testimony moves from local to imperial through the very opposition intended to suppress it.
8: 'Be it known to the king that we went to the province of Judah, to the house of the great God.' The imperial officials' own language — 'the great God' — reflects the reputation of YHWH reaching the Gentile administration. The pagan witness to YHWH's greatness runs through the OT from Rahab to Cyrus to these administrators. Christ's mission is to bring this Gentile acknowledgment to its fullest form: 'every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father' (Philippians 2:11).
9: 'We then asked those elders and spoke to them thus: Who gave you a decree to build this house and to finish this timber work?' The repeated question of authorization — each new official asks the same thing. The authorization for covenant building activity is always questioned by those who did not initiate it. Christ's authorization is from the Father: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18) — the question of authorization finds its ultimate answer in the resurrection.
10: 'We also asked them their names, for your information, that we might write down the names of the men at their head.' The imperial registry of the covenant community's leaders. The names of Zerubbabel and Jeshua are being entered into a Persian administrative record. Christ promises a different registry: 'I will confess his name before my Father and before his angels' (Revelation 3:5) — the names of the faithful entered into the eternal record that no imperial edict can expunge.
11: 'And this was their reply to us: We are the servants of the God of heaven and earth, and we are rebuilding the house that was built many years ago, which a great king of Israel built and finished.' The builders' self-identification: servants of the God of heaven and earth. The claim goes beyond local religious identity to cosmic authority — the God of heaven and earth built the institution that heaven's servants are now rebuilding. Christ's mission rests on the same cosmic authorization: 'my Father is working until now, and I am working' (John 5:17).
12: 'But because our fathers had angered the God of heaven, he gave them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, the Chaldean, who destroyed this house and carried away the people to Babylonia.' The builders' honest confession of why the house was destroyed — covenant faithlessness, divine judgment, exile. The same explanatory honesty governs the NT's account of Israel's history (Acts 7:51-53). Christ does not paper over the history of covenant failure; he enters into it to resolve it.
13: 'However, in the first year of Cyrus king of Babylon, Cyrus the king made a decree that this house of God should be rebuilt.' The pivot: however, Cyrus. The counter-movement to the covenant judgment is initiated by a pagan king acting as YHWH's instrument. Isaiah had named Cyrus a century and a half before (Isaiah 44:28; Ezra 45:1) — the specific prophecy of the pagan ruler who would say 'let Jerusalem be built.' Christ is the fulfillment of the greater return: 'the year of the LORD's favor' (Isaiah 61:2, Luke 4:19) that Cyrus's decree anticipated.
14: The gold and silver vessels from the temple that Nebuchadnezzar had taken were handed over to Sheshbazzar the governor whom Cyrus had appointed. The return of the temple vessels is the tangible form of covenant restoration — the sacred objects removed in judgment are returned when the judgment is lifted. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate return of what was taken: 'you took him by lawless hands... but God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:23-24). What death took, resurrection returns.
15: 'And he said to him, Take these vessels, go and put them in the temple that is in Jerusalem, and let the house of God be rebuilt on its site.' The restoration command — sacred objects returned to their proper place. Christ restores humanity to its proper place in covenant relationship: 'no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God' (Ephesians 2:19). The displaced made proper again, the vessels returned to the temple.
16: Then this Sheshbazzar came and laid the foundations of the house of God that is in Jerusalem, and from that time until now it has been in building, and it is not yet finished. The long duration between foundation-laying and completion — the building begun but not yet finished at the time of writing. The church is similarly in the same state: 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone' (Ephesians 2:20) — the foundation laid, the building ongoing, completion awaiting the eschatological consummation.
17: 'Now therefore, if it seems good to the king, let search be made in the royal archives there in Babylon, to see whether a decree was issued by Cyrus the king for the rebuilding of this house of God in Jerusalem.' The builders appeal to the existing decree — the prior authorization already on record. Christ appeals to the prior authorization of Scripture: 'these are my words that I spoke to you while I was still with you, that everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled' (Luke 24:44). The covenant's authorization has always been on record.
